mnemonics.title: Page Replacement Algorithms
mnemonics.content:
FIFO: FIRST IN FIRST OUT - OLDEST PAGE LEAVES FIRST
LRU: LEAST RECENTLY USED - OLDEST ACCESS TIME GOES
OPTIMAL: LOOKS FORWARD - PAGE NOT USED LONGEST WINS
CLOCK: CIRCULAR QUEUE - USED BITS ROTATE THROUGH

acronyms.usage: Hardware cache for virtual-to-physical address translation, speeds up memory access
Contains frame number, valid bit, dirty bit, reference bit, and access rights for a virtual page
Address generated by CPU, split into page number and offset
Actual address in RAM, used to access physical memory
OS technique to provide illusion of larger memory using disk and RAM

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table with virtual page number as row index. Each row contains: Frame number, Valid bit (V/I), Dirty bit (M), Reference bit (R), Protection bits grid. A simple shows the mapping clearly.

Think of TLB as a small associative array next to CPU. When address comes, parallel search in TLB AND page number. If found (hit), get frame directly. If not (miss), walk page table then cache result in TLB.
Draw a U-shaped curve: As degree of multiprogramming increases, CPU utilization rises initially, then drops sharply when thrashing begins. The turning point is optimal multiprogramming level.
Imagine pages arranged in a circle (clock face). A pointer scans through. If reference bit is 0, replace that page. If 1, clear bit and move on. One full rotation may be needed.

analogies.analogy: Like a library's off-site storage system. You (CPU) request a book (data) from your desk (memory). If it's at your desk, instant access. If at storage (disk), request takes time. You don't know where the book actually is.
Like looking for a book in your personal library and finding an empty shelf with a 'waiting for delivery' slip. The librarian (OS) must fetch it from the basement storage (disk).
Like a frequently-called contacts list in your phone. Instead of searching entire phonebook (page table in memory), you check quick-dial first (TLB). Most calls go through fast.
Like a student with too many subjects to study but only room for one book on the desk. Constantly swapping books in/out leaves no time for actual studying (computation).
